posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked:
I had the CC and the Petits Monts last week in France. The CC was extraordinary. The style is more to my taste than the Monty (not to say I dislike the Monty). It’s far more incisive and focused and in 19 had zero botrytis. After 24 hours in the decanter it continued to flesh out and but maintain a strong acid core.

I would post a picture of the bottles, the table cloth will probably give away the location ! Sadly though, I seem incapable of uploading the file

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:

It’s far more incisive and focused and in 19 had zero botrytis. After 24 hours in the decanter it continued to flesh out and but maintain a strong acid core.

I would post a picture of the bottles, the table cloth will probably give away the location ! Sadly though, I seem incapable of uploading the file

I’ve seen the Petits Monte in pictures, but wondering if it was released? If so, where? I assume production was tiny, but wasn’t sure what the winery did with the wine.

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked:
The PM was released mostly to restaurants in France in the same way that the Duvault-Blochet is distributed. The 2019 that I drank was one of 847 bottles. I believe there is an ‘understanding’ with The Domaine that the price will be kept fair and that no bottles should appear on the secondary market. I paid €850 at the restaurant. There are a few available on Europe brokers at €5k. The Domaine also asks that empty bottles are returned to them, hence I wasn’t able to bring the empties home. This helps control resale as well as counterfeiting.

The wine drank brilliantly. Amazingly red fruited.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: For anyone who has had this, how does it compare to BdM?

2019 Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru, Domaine de la Romanée-Conti 97 *****
There are four plots one lower down, one above and two in the middle, all vinified and matured separately. During elevage the middle plots seemed to be the best, the lower section good, while the wine from the top block appeared to be too acid. But it is now clear to Aubert that the blend needed them all, including the top, which was raised in one year old wood, the rest in new barrels. The wine took its time, both for the alcoholic and malolactic fermentations, so the Corton-Charlemagne was bottled later than the Montrachet. A first bottle was extrovert but soon evidence of a slight cork taint appeared. The second bottle was clean, if not quite so immediately exuberant. This is chiselled and fine with wonderful length. The stones are more apparent and they are washed with a fresh citrus. Clean pure and classy with real grip, there is power behind and the fruit totally fills out the back of the palate. A brilliant first offering of Corton-Charlemagne. Tasted: November 2021

TN: Twelve Vintages of Lafon Montrachet WINE TALK
TWELVE VINTAGES OF LAFON MONTRACHET - (10/6/2023) We had an exceptional dinner Friday, which I organized to taste through and contrast the recent decade of Lafon Montrachet. We added 2000 and 2005 to make it an even dozen. I'm an unabashed fan of all things Lafon, and own and buy their whites across vintages and across the lineup. And over the last ten to fifteen years this impressive lineup has continued to improve, going from one level of greatness to another. At the top of that mountain is …
